title: Homemade Pizza Dough Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Pizza Dough Amounts by Number of Guests
description: 🍕 Homemade pizza dough: 250g flour for medium pizza. Scale by number of guests · Free & fast.
intro: This calculator tells you exactly how many grams of pizza dough to prepare based on your guest count and pizza size. The base formula used by professional pizzaiolos is **250 g of dough per 12-inch (30 cm) personal pizza**, scaling linearly with size and guest count. Whether you're hosting a backyard party for 20 or a family dinner for 4, getting the dough weight right is the difference between running out of pizza and wasting ingredients. Dough weight directly controls crust thickness, diameter, and yield — so precision matters before you even touch the flour.
keyTakeaway: **Total Dough (g) = Guests × Dough-per-Pizza (g)**, where a 10-inch pizza = 200 g, 12-inch = 250 g, 14-inch = 320 g, and 16-inch = 430 g of raw dough per pie.
useCases: Planning a birthday party: scaling dough for 15 guests each getting one 12-inch personal pizza (= 3,750 g total, roughly 7.5 cups of flour)
Meal prep Sunday: calculating dough batches for 4 family members with two 14-inch pies split between them
Catering event: determining how many 1-kg dough balls to pre-make for a 50-guest pizza station with a mix of sizes
Restaurant trial run: benchmarking raw dough weight per pizza size against Neapolitan VPN standards (200–280 g for a 30 cm pie)
explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator uses dough ball weight benchmarks established by professional pizza associations and widely adopted in bakery science. The core formula is:

```
Total Dough (g) = Number of Guests × Dough Weight per Pizza (g)

Dough Weight per Pizza (g) = π × r² × TF

Where:
  r  = radius of the pizza in inches
  TF = Thickness Factor (0.10 for thin crust, 0.13 for classic, 0.16 for thick/pan)
```

**Example — Classic 12-inch crust:**
```
r = 6 inches
TF = 0.13 (classic/NY style)
Dough = 3.14159 × 6² × 0.13 = 3.14159 × 36 × 0.13 ≈ 147 g  (in oz-based units)
```
> Note: The TF formula gives results in ounces when using inch measurements; multiply by 28.35 to convert to grams. Professional apps round to practical benchmarks (250 g for 12-inch classic), which this calculator uses for home-kitchen accuracy.

---

## Reference Table

| Pizza Size | Diameter | Thin Crust | Classic Crust | Thick/Pan Crust |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Personal | 8 in / 20 cm | 120 g | 150 g | 185 g |
| Small | 10 in / 25 cm | 160 g | 200 g | 250 g |
| Medium | 12 in / 30 cm | 200 g | 250 g | 310 g |
| Large | 14 in / 35 cm | 255 g | 320 g | 395 g |
| XL / Party | 16 in / 40 cm | 340 g | 430 g | 530 g |

> Benchmarks align with Associazione Verace Pizza Napoletana (VPN) standard of 200–280 g for a 30 cm Neapolitan pizza.

---

## Typical Use Cases with Real Numbers

### Case 1 — Family Pizza Night (4 guests, 12-inch classic)
- Pizzas needed: 2 pies (feeds 4 adults, ~3 slices each)
- Dough per pie: 250 g
- **Total dough: 500 g**
- Flour needed (at 60% hydration): ≈ 312 g flour + 187 g water + 10 g salt + 3 g yeast

### Case 2 — Birthday Party (12 guests, 14-inch classic, 1 pizza per 3 guests)
- Pizzas needed: 4 pies
- Dough per pie: 320 g
- **Total dough: 1,280 g**
- Plan for ~2 standard batches (each batch ≈ 650 g dough from 400 g flour)

### Case 3 — Large Event (30 guests, 16-inch thin crust, buffet style)
- Pizzas needed: 10 pies (buffet portions = ~4 slices per person, 8 slices per 16-inch pie)
- Dough per pie: 340 g
- **Total dough: 3,400 g**
- Prep tip: Divide into 10 × 340 g balls, cold-ferment in fridge 24–72 hours for best flavor

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Forgetting that dough loses ~10–15% weight during baking** — always base your guest math on raw dough weight; the baked crust will be lighter but that's already factored into the size benchmarks above.
2. **Using volume (cups) instead of weight (grams)** — flour compaction can vary by up to 30% depending on how it's scooped. A kitchen scale is non-negotiable for consistent results.
3. **Assuming 1 pizza feeds everyone equally** — children eat less (½ pizza), hungry adults may eat more (1.5 pizzas). Add a 15–20% buffer for mixed-age groups.
4. **Underestimating hydration absorption by flour type** — All-purpose flour (11–12% protein) absorbs less water than bread flour (12–14% protein) or 00 Italian flour. Using the wrong flour at the same hydration % changes the dough's final weight and texture significantly.
5. **Not accounting for dough rest and fermentation expansion** — Dough increases in volume (not weight) during fermentation. A 250 g ball will still weigh 250 g after rising, but will be much easier to stretch. Don't add extra flour after fermentation thinking it's "too sticky."

---

## Related Calculators

- Explore baking ratios with the [Flour Hydration Calculator](/flour-hydration-calculator)
- Scale up ingredient quantities with the [Recipe Multiplier Calculator](/recipe-multiplier)
- Estimate cooking time adjustments using the [Oven Temperature Converter](/oven-temperature-converter)

faq.q: How many grams of dough do I need per person for pizza?
faq.a: For a standard 12-inch (30 cm) pizza — which typically serves 2–3 adults — you need 250 g of raw dough. That works out to roughly 83–125 g of dough per person. For a generous single-person portion (personal 10-inch pizza), budget 200 g of dough per guest.

faq.q: What is the baker's percentage for pizza dough hydration?
faq.a: Classic Neapolitan dough uses 55–60% hydration (water weight ÷ flour weight × 100). New York style sits at 58–65%. High-hydration doughs (70–80%) produce an airy, open crumb but are harder to handle. USDA dietary guidelines don't regulate this, but food science bakery literature consistently uses 60–65% as the home-baker sweet spot.

faq.q: How many pizzas do I need for 10 guests?
faq.a: A rule of thumb used by catering professionals is 3–4 slices per adult, with a standard pizza cut into 8 slices. For 10 guests at 3 slices each: 30 slices ÷ 8 = 3.75 pizzas — round up to 4 pies. If guests are heavy eaters or the event is dinner (not lunch), plan 5 pizzas to be safe.

faq.q: Can I make the dough ahead of time and refrigerate it?
faq.a: Yes — cold fermentation (24–72 hours at 38–40°F / 3–4°C) is highly recommended. The slow fermentation improves flavor by developing organic acids and complex carbohydrates. Food safety guidelines from the USDA and FDA note that raw dough should not sit at room temperature for more than 2 hours (in the danger zone of 40–140°F). Pre-made dough balls stored in oiled airtight containers keep well for up to 3 days refrigerated or 3 months frozen.

faq.q: What flour type is best for pizza dough, and does it change the gram amounts?
faq.a: "00" Italian flour (very finely milled, ~11.5% protein) is the Neapolitan standard and yields a soft, extensible dough. Bread flour (12–14% protein) is preferred for NY-style due to its chew. All-purpose flour works in a pinch. The gram amounts in this calculator don't change by flour type — the dough ball weight is a fixed target — but hydration % may need adjustment: 00 flour typically needs 55–60% water, bread flour 60–65%.

faq.q: How does pizza size affect the thickness factor and dough weight?
faq.a: The Thickness Factor (TF) is a constant derived from the desired dough density per square inch. Using TF = 0.13 (NY classic style): a 10-inch pizza has 78.5 in² of surface area × 0.13 = ~10.2 oz ≈ 289 g, while a 14-inch pizza has 153.9 in² × 0.13 = ~20 oz ≈ 567 g. Practical pizza-making rounds these to usable benchmarks (250 g and 320 g respectively) because home ovens and stretch technique introduce variability.

faq.q: How much dough should I prepare if some guests have dietary restrictions?
faq.a: If you're serving gluten-free guests, prepare their dough separately using a 1:1 GF flour blend — note that GF doughs typically absorb 10–15% more liquid than wheat flour. Budget the same gram weight per pizza (250 g for 12-inch). For vegan guests, standard pizza dough is already vegan (flour, water, salt, yeast). Always add a 20% buffer on total dough when catering to mixed dietary needs to avoid running short on any one variety.

faq.q: What is the standard yeast amount for pizza dough per gram of flour?
faq.a: For active dry yeast, the standard ratio is 1% of flour weight (baker's percentage). So for 500 g of flour, use 5 g of active dry yeast. For instant yeast, use 0.75% (3.75 g per 500 g flour). For a slow cold fermentation (48–72 hours), reduce yeast to 0.1–0.2% to avoid over-fermentation. Salt should be 2–2.5% of flour weight — salt inhibits yeast, so never mix them directly before adding water.
